inn: trivalent influenza vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture)

4.1.title: Therapeutic indications
4.1.text:
Supemtek is indicated for active immunisation for the prevention of influenza disease in adults and children from 9 years of age and older.

Supemtek should be used in accordance with official recommendations.

4.2.title: Posology and method of administration
4.2.text:
Posology

Adults and children from 9 years of age One dose of 0.5 mL.

Paediatric population The safety and efficacy of Supemtek have not been established in individuals below 3 years of age. Current available safety and immunogenicity data of Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) in children from 3 to less than 8 years of age are described in section 5.1 but no recommendation on a posology can be made.

Method of administration

For intramuscular injection only. The preferred site is in the deltoid muscle. The vaccine must not be injected intravascularly and must not be mixed with other vaccines in the same syringe.

For instructions on the handling of the vaccine before administration, see section 6.6.

4.3.title: Contraindications
4.3.text: Hypersensitivity to the active substances, to any of the excipients listed in section 6.1 or to any trace residues such as octylphenol ethoxylate.

4.4.title: Special warnings and precautions for use
4.4.text:
Traceability

In order to improve the traceability of biological medicinal products, the name and the batch number of the administered product should be clearly recorded.

Hypersensitivity and anaphylaxis

Appropriate medical treatment and supervision should always be readily available in case of an anaphylactic event following the administration of the vaccine.

Concurrent illness

Vaccination should be postponed in patients with acute febrile illness until the fever is resolved.

Immunodeficiency

Antibody response in patients with endogenous or iatrogenic immunosuppression may be insufficient to prevent influenza.

Thrombocytopenia and coagulation disorders

As with all injectable vaccines, Supemtek must be administered with caution to individuals with thrombocytopenia or a bleeding disorder since bleeding may occur following an intramuscular administration to these subjects.

Syncope

Syncope can occur following or even before any vaccination as a psychogenic response to the needle injection. This can be accompanied by several neurological signs such as transient visual disturbance, paraesthesia and tonic-clonic limb movements during recovery. Procedures should be in place to prevent falling and injury and to manage syncope.

Protection

As with any vaccine, vaccination with Supemtek may not protect all vaccinees.

Excipients with known effect

Sodium content This medicinal product contains less than 1 mmol sodium (23 mg) per dose, that is to say is essentially “sodium free”.

Polysorbate 20 content This medicinal product contains 0.0275 mg of polysorbate 20 (E 432) in each 0.5 mL dose. Polysorbates may cause allergic reactions.

4.8.title: Undesirable effects
4.8.text:
Data for Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) are relevant to Supemtek because both vaccines are manufactured using the same process and have overlapping compositions.

Summary of the safety profile

In participants 18 to 49 years of age the most common injection-site adverse reactions occurring after Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) administration were tenderness (48.0%) and pain (36.8%); most common systemic adverse reactions were headache (20.3.%), fatigue (16.5%) and muscle pain (12.8%). In participants 50 years of age and older the most common injection-site adverse reactions were tenderness (34.3%) and pain (18.9%) and the most common systemic adverse reactions were headache (12.7%) and fatigue (12.2%).

In children/adolescents 9 to 17 years of age who received Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture), the most common injection-site adverse reaction was pain (34.4%). The most common systemic adverse reactions were myalgia (19.3%), headache (18.5%) and malaise (16.1%).

The severity of the adverse reactions was mild to moderate. Onset usually occurred within the first 3 days after vaccination. All resolved without sequelae.

Tabulated list of adverse reactions

The adverse reactions are listed by MedDRA system organ class under headings of frequency using the following convention: Very common (≥ 1/10); Common (≥ 1/100 to < 1/10); Uncommon (≥ 1/1 000 to < 1/100); Rare (≥ 1/10 000 to < 1/1 000); Very rare (< 1/10 000); Frequency not known (cannot be estimated from the available data). Within each frequency grouping, adverse reactions are presented in the order of decreasing seriousness.

Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) has been administered to and safety data collected from 998 adults 18-49 years of age (study 1), 4 328 adults 50 years of age and older (study 2), and from 658 adults 18-49 years of age (study 3) and from 641 children/adolescents 9-17 years of age (study 3).

Reporting of suspected adverse reactions

Reporting suspected adverse reactions after authorisation of the medicinal product is important. It allows continued monitoring of the benefit/risk balance of the medicinal product. Healthcare professionals are asked to report any suspected adverse reactions via the national reporting system listed in Appendix V.

5.1.title: Pharmacodynamic properties
5.1.text:
Pharmacotherapeutic group: Vaccines, influenza vaccine, ATC code: J07BB02

Mechanism of action

Supemtek contains recombinant HA proteins of the three strains of influenza virus specified by health authorities for inclusion in the annual seasonal vaccine. These proteins function as antigens which induce a humoral immune response, measured by hemagglutination inhibition (HI) antibody that is known to protect against influenza infection.

Antibodies against one influenza virus type or subtype confer limited or no protection against another. Furthermore, antibodies to one antigenic variant of influenza virus might not protect against a new antigenic variant of the same type or subtype. Frequent development of antigenic variants through antigenic drift is the virologic basis for seasonal epidemics and the reason for the usual replacement of one or more influenza virus strains in each year's influenza vaccine. Therefore, influenza vaccines are standardized to contain the hemagglutinins of influenza virus strains (i.e. typically two type A and one type B), representing the influenza viruses likely to be circulating in the upcoming season.

Clinical efficacy

The efficacy and immunogenicity of Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) is relevant to Supemtek because both vaccines are manufactured using the same process and have overlapping compositions.

Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) efficacy in terms of prevention of laboratory-confirmed influenza-like illness (ILI) caused by any strain of influenza, was evaluated in adults ≥ 50 years of age and conducted during the 2014-2015 influenza season in the United States (study 2).

A total of 8 963 healthy, medically stable adults were randomised in a 1:1 ratio to receive a single dose of Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) (n=4 474) or an egg- based quadrivalent inactivated influenza vaccine (IIV4) (n=4 489). A total of 5 412 (60.4%) subjects

were 50-64 years of age, 2 532 (28.2%) were 65-74 years of age and 1 019 (11.4 %) were ≥ 75 years of age. The primary efficacy endpoint of study 2 was reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (rtPCR)-positive, protocol-defined ILI due to any strain of influenza.

Laboratory-confirmed protocol defined ILI was defined as having at least one symptom in each of two categories of respiratory and systemic symptoms, which could include sore throat, cough, sputum production, wheezing and difficulty breathing, or systemic symptoms such as fever > 37°C, chills, fatigue, headache and myalgia, laboratory-confirmed by rtPCR. US epidemiological data for the 2014-2015 influenza season indicated that Influenza A (H3N2) viruses predominated and that most influenza A/H3N2 viruses were antigenically dissimilar while A/H1N1 and B viruses were antigenically similar to vaccine antigens. Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) met the pre-specified success criterion for non-inferiority to the comparator pre-defined as a lower bound of the two-sided 95% CI > -20%.

Immunogenicity

Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) was evaluated in healthy adults of 18-49 years of age in a randomised, observer-blind, active controlled, non-inferiority immunogenicity, multi-centre study conducted during the 2014-2015 influenza season in the United States (study 1).

In study 1, subjects received Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) (N=998) or an egg-based quadrivalent inactivated influenza vaccine (IIV4) (N=332). Immunogenicity was assessed before and 28 days after administration of a single dose of study vaccine.

Haemagglutination inhibition (HAI) geometric mean titres (GMTs) were determined for the two vaccine groups for each vaccine antigen. Immunogenicity was compared by calculating the difference in seroconversion rates (SCR) and the ratios of GMTs of Comparator to Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture).

Study 1 had two co-primary endpoints: GMTs and day 28 HAI seroconversion rates for each of the four antigens contained in the study vaccines.

Quadrivalent Influenza Vaccine (recombinant, prepared in cell culture) met the success criterion for GMTs for three of the four antigens but did not meet the success criteria for the B/Victoria lineage antigen (Table 3). Antibody titres against the B/Victoria were low in both vaccine groups.
